title: Branch Closing

jokes.text: Jim reveals he's been sending Dwight faxes from himself in the future about poisoned coffee
jokes.setup: Jim explains a mysterious faxing situation to the camera
jokes.punchline: The reveal that he's pranking Dwight with time-travel warnings
jokes.explanation: Classic Jim prank with perfect character integration - only Jim would execute this elaborate long-distance psychological warfare on Dwight. The time travel angle is creative and the execution is beautifully methodical.

jokes.text: Dwight knocking coffee out of someone's hand shouting 'Nooo!' then saying 'You'll thank me later'
jokes.setup: Jim's future warning about poisoned coffee
jokes.punchline: Dwight taking the prank seriously and dramatically 'saving' someone
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to the prank setup. Dwight's complete sincerity in 'saving' someone makes it funnier. The physical comedy of knocking coffee away is classic Office.

jokes.text: I love to start my day with a hearty bowl of Jan. Just call me Levinson in the morning, baby.
jokes.setup: Jan Levinson arrives at the office
jokes.punchline: Michael's inappropriate sexual wordplay with her name
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael inappropriate humor. Well-integrated for his character but not particularly original or clever. Gets a cringe laugh but relies on sexual innuendo.

jokes.text: On whom's authority? The board's.
jokes.setup: Michael asking who authorized the branch closing
jokes.punchline: Jan's matter-of-fact response to his grammatically incorrect formal question
jokes.explanation: Michael's attempt at formal language ('whom's') combined with Jan's deadpan response. Character-appropriate but minor moment.

jokes.text: Am I a small number person or a severance package person? But you're a severance package person.
jokes.setup: Michael asking about his fate in the restructuring
jokes.punchline: Jan's blunt confirmation that he's getting fired
jokes.explanation: Great cringe moment. Michael's euphemistic language contrasted with Jan's brutal directness. The clinical 'severance package person' phrasing is darkly funny.

jokes.text: Call me. You got it, buddy.
jokes.setup: Someone asking Kevin to call if he finds out anything about the situation
jokes.punchline: Kevin's overly earnest 'buddy' response to what's clearly a serious situation
jokes.explanation: Kevin's characteristic inappropriate tone-deafness. Minor character moment that fits his personality but not particularly strong.

jokes.text: King of the stupid universe?
jokes.setup: Jan explaining Josh will play an important role in the company's future
jokes.punchline: Michael's childish insult disguised as a question
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael jealousy and immaturity. The formal question format makes the childish insult funnier.

jokes.text: I know I hurt you. But please do not do this to me. Don't hurt me like I hurt you.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to convince Jan not to close the branch
jokes.punchline: His assumption that this business decision is personal revenge
jokes.explanation: Excellent cringe comedy. Michael's narcissistic assumption that corporate restructuring is about his personal relationship with Jan. Peak Michael Scott delusion.

jokes.text: You think Stanleys grow on trees? Well, they don't. There is no Stanley tree.
jokes.setup: Michael defending his employees to the camera
jokes.punchline: The literal interpretation of the 'growing on trees' metaphor applied to Stanley
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd Michael logic. Taking a common expression literally and applying it to a specific employee creates perfect absurdist humor. Highly quotable and rewatchable.

jokes.text: Show me that farm. With Phyllises and Kevins sprouting up all over the place, ripe for the plucking. Show me that farm.
jokes.setup: Michael continuing his 'employees don't grow on trees' rant
jokes.punchline: Extending the metaphor to a farm with employees as crops 'ripe for plucking'
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of the Stanley tree concept. The agricultural metaphor gets more elaborate and absurd. 'Ripe for the plucking' is a beautiful touch. Michael's passion makes it even funnier.

jokes.text: When you become close with someone, you develop a kind of sixth sense. You can read their moods like a book. Right now, the title of Michael's book is Something Weird Is Going On. Colon: What Did Jan Say?
jokes.setup: Dwight explaining his connection to Michael
jokes.punchline: The overly specific 'book title' format for reading Michael's mood
jokes.explanation: Classic Dwight over-explanation with absurd specificity. The book title format is creative and the colon addition shows his pedantic nature perfectly.

jokes.text: The Michael Scott Story. By Michael Scott. With Dwight Schrute.
jokes.setup: Dwight continuing the book metaphor
jokes.punchline: Adding full authorship credits including himself as co-author
jokes.explanation: Nice escalation of the book metaphor. Dwight inserting himself as co-author of Michael's emotional state is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Rumor has it that the Scranton branch is 'gklch.' In your face. Sucka.
jokes.setup: Jim hearing about Scranton's troubles
jokes.punchline: Making a throat-slitting gesture while saying 'gklch' then immediately gloating
jokes.explanation: Jim's childish gloating is funny given his complicated feelings about Scranton. The throat-cutting sound effect adds visual comedy.

jokes.text: Are you okay? Yeah. Great, amazing. Best physical condition of my life.
jokes.setup: Pam asking if Michael is okay after the devastating news
jokes.punchline: Michael's obvious lie about being in great condition
jokes.explanation: Michael's defensive deflection is believable but not particularly clever. The physical condition claim is absurdly specific for the situation.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, you walk so slowly.
jokes.setup: Michael asking Toby into his office during a crisis
jokes.punchline: Michael's impatient complaint about Toby's walking speed
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael-Toby dynamic. Even in a crisis, Michael finds time to be unnecessarily mean to Toby. Economy of language makes it funnier.

jokes.text: It makes perfect sense that it would happen today because I just received this in the mail. 1,000 business cards with this address and phone number.
jokes.setup: Andy reflecting on the timing of the branch closing
jokes.punchline: The cruel irony of receiving new business cards on the day the branch closes
jokes.explanation: Good situational irony. The specificity of 1,000 cards makes the waste more painful and funny. Relatable workplace frustration.

jokes.text: If I get to stay and Ryan is laid off, I will kill myself. Like Romeo and Juliet. The Claire Danes one.
jokes.setup: Kelly worried about the restructuring
jokes.punchline: Her dramatic suicide threat with specific movie reference
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kelly - dramatic, pop culture obsessed, and backwards logic (killing herself if she gets to stay). The Claire Danes specification is a nice touch.

jokes.text: It'd be like going to your high school reunion, and saying 'Hey, I missed you guys.' And then they're all like, 'Oh, don't worry about it. We're all gonna move in with you... forever.'
jokes.setup: Josh commenting on Scranton employees potentially transferring
jokes.punchline: The extended metaphor about unwanted permanent houseguests
jokes.explanation: Clever extended metaphor about workplace integration. The 'forever' tag at the end escalates it nicely. Good observational humor about office dynamics.

jokes.text: So listen, I know you're seeing someone, but I'm still willing if you are. What... willing? You remember. Like six years ago we said that on our last day of work that we would... sleep together. Oh... God, uh, um, I did not say that. Was that not you? No, not... not me.
jokes.setup: Someone approaching Meredith with what they think is a prior agreement
jokes.punchline: The revelation that he's completely misremembered or misunderstood the situation
jokes.explanation: Excellent cringe comedy. The slow revelation that he's completely wrong creates sustained discomfort. Meredith's confusion adds to the awkwardness.

jokes.text: Day just gets worse and worse.
jokes.setup: The awkward sexual proposition that went nowhere
jokes.punchline: Meredith's understated reaction to the uncomfortable encounter
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan reaction. Meredith treating the sexual rejection as just another minor inconvenience in an already bad day is funny understatement.

jokes.text: It's a blessing in disguise. Actually, not even in disguise.
jokes.setup: Stanley commenting on the branch closing
jokes.punchline: Correcting the idiom to be more direct about his happiness
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly economical wordplay. Stanley's literal-minded correction of the idiom perfectly captures his desire to leave. The phrase structure is clever and quotable.

jokes.text: In my fantasy, I always thought I would slap someone, make a big speech and storm out forever. But this is good too.
jokes.setup: Stanley explaining his satisfaction with the situation
jokes.punchline: His admission that getting fired is almost as good as his dramatic exit fantasy
jokes.explanation: Excellent character revelation. Stanley's secret fantasy of a dramatic exit contrasted with his practical satisfaction with getting fired. Perfect Stanley - pragmatic but with hidden depths.

jokes.text: I don't wanna work here without Pam. That'd just be like loading trucks without any meaning. You know?
jokes.setup: Roy expressing concern about the changes
jokes.punchline: His comparison of work without Pam to meaningless truck loading
jokes.explanation: Sweet but clumsy Roy sentiment. The truck-loading metaphor is endearingly inarticulate and very Roy. Shows his limited emotional vocabulary.

jokes.text: I couldn't be happier. I'm gonna take the severance and retire. My wife and I are gonna travel. I really couldn't be happier.
jokes.setup: Stanley's reaction to potential job loss
jokes.punchline: His genuine enthusiasm about retirement, repeated for emphasis
jokes.explanation: Stanley's continued satisfaction with the situation. The repetition of 'couldn't be happier' emphasizes his genuine joy. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: I wasn't. So you'll be okay too. You're a warrior. You're smart, capable.
jokes.setup: Michael saying not to worry about him, he'll land on his feet
jokes.punchline: Darryl's blunt 'I wasn't' in response to 'don't worry about me'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Darryl deadpan. The brutal honesty of 'I wasn't' followed immediately by generic encouragement is expertly timed. Great economy and character voice.

jokes.text: Actually, Bob Vance bought out the warehouse. So he's keeping on the whole crew. So we're good.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to comfort Darryl about finding work
jokes.punchline: Darryl revealing he's actually fine while Michael is the one in trouble
jokes.explanation: Good reversal. Michael's attempt at comfort backfires when Darryl reveals he's secure. The casual delivery makes Michael's situation seem worse.

jokes.text: This is my house. The CFO's taking away my house and giving it to Josh. And Josh is giving the garage to Bob Vance.
jokes.setup: Michael processing the corporate restructuring
jokes.punchline: His metaphor of the office as his house being subdivided
jokes.explanation: Excellent metaphor that captures Michael's sense of ownership and betrayal. The house/garage division makes the corporate restructuring more relatable and personal.

jokes.text: Um, like when you said the branch was closing?
jokes.setup: Michael saying some may have heard rumors about the branch closing
jokes.punchline: Employee pointing out that Michael himself announced it
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Michael's earlier announcement. The deadpan correction of his 'rumors' euphemism is effective workplace comedy.

jokes.text: Shotgun! No, there's no one else. Still.
jokes.setup: Dwight and Michael preparing to drive to New York
jokes.punchline: Dwight calling shotgun when he's the only passenger
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight logic - following protocol even when it's pointless. His 'Still' shows he's aware of the absurdity but committed to the form.

jokes.text: You send him cards? You've never met him. Well, when I do, we'll have something to talk about.
jokes.setup: Michael revealing he has the CFO's address from his Christmas card list
jokes.punchline: His logic for sending cards to someone he's never met
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Michael logic. Sending Christmas cards as a conversation starter for a meeting that might never happen is perfectly absurd and optimistic.

jokes.text: I think it's for the best.
jokes.setup: Ryan talking about his and Kelly's relationship ending due to the changes
jokes.punchline: His understated relief at escaping the relationship
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ryan emotional detachment. His relief at escaping Kelly is understated but clear. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: This is exactly what Michael Moore does. Famous documentarian. He goes up to people with a camera, and he's like, 'Why did you do this? Why did you pollute? You are bad. You're a bad person.' It's very dramatic. Although I can't say I was a big fan of Bowling For Columbine. Because I thought it was gonna be a bowling movie. Like Kingpin. And it wasn't. It was something else.
jokes.setup: Michael explaining his plan to confront the CFO
jokes.punchline: His complete misunderstanding of what Bowling for Columbine was about
jokes.explanation: Excellent Michael cultural ignorance. Expecting a documentary about a school shooting to be like Kingpin is perfectly absurd. Great character-specific humor about his assumptions.

jokes.text: I, um... will not be taking the job. W... Excu... why not? As of today, I have accepted a senior management position at Staples. Today.
jokes.setup: Josh being offered the expanded role he supposedly wanted
jokes.punchline: His revelation that he's leaving for Staples, using the offer as leverage
jokes.explanation: Great plot twist and corporate betrayal. Josh using his promotion as leverage to get a better job elsewhere is believably ruthless. Jan's stuttering reaction sells it.

jokes.text: This whole restructuring thing was based around keeping you. Damn it, Josh.
jokes.setup: Josh's betrayal of the company's trust
jokes.punchline: Jan's exasperated admission that the entire plan was built around Josh
jokes.explanation: Good corporate frustration. The revelation that the whole plan was Josh-centric makes his betrayal more devastating. Jan's 'Damn it' is uncharacteristically unprofessional.

jokes.text: Say what you will about Michael Scott. But he would never do that.
jokes.setup: Josh's corporate betrayal
jokes.punchline: Jim's backhanded defense of Michael's loyalty
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Jim observation. Defending Michael through his incompetence rather than his virtues is perfectly crafted. 'Say what you will' sets up the backhanded nature perfectly.

jokes.text: Listen, Scott. It's no longer financially viable. We're losing money, okay? It's not a charity. It's a business. And it's a dying business. Look, the whole business model of the small regional paper company simply doesn't make sense anymore. Stop... stop it. Just... Okay, he's not gonna say any of that.
jokes.setup: Dwight role-playing as the CFO in practice
jokes.punchline: Dwight delivering a devastating and realistic business analysis that Michael can't handle
jokes.explanation: Excellent role reversal. Dwight being more realistic and devastating than Michael expects. The business analysis is too accurate for Michael to handle, showing his denial.

jokes.text: Don't touch me this time.
jokes.setup: Michael wanting to restart their practice session
jokes.punchline: Reference to Dwight apparently touching him during the previous attempt
jokes.explanation: Minor callback to their practice session. Implies physical comedy we didn't see. Typical Michael-Dwight boundary issues.

jokes.text: Kelly, best wishes. Love, Pam. P.S. What a long, strange trip it's been.
jokes.setup: Kelly asking Pam to sign her company directory
jokes.punchline: Pam's Grateful Dead reference for a mundane office situation
jokes.explanation: Nice Pam humor - applying countercultural lyrics to corporate life. Shows her subtle wit and ability to find irony in mundane situations.

jokes.text: No, no, no. Just, um, some personal stuff. And I'm not really ready to revisit that, I don't think.
jokes.setup: Jan offering Jim the Scranton job
jokes.punchline: Jim's awkward deflection about 'personal stuff' preventing his return
jokes.explanation: Good Jim emotional deflection. His discomfort about returning to Pam's proximity is understated but clear. Fits his conflict avoidance pattern.

jokes.text: Hey, did we ever have a conversation about doing something on the last day of work? Does that ring a bell? Do you remember hearing a rumor about me and anybody, last day of work? Something sexual? No.
jokes.setup: Michael's desperate attempt to find the source of the earlier sexual proposition
jokes.punchline: His increasingly specific and awkward questioning of Meredith
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the earlier sexual proposition mistake. Michael's detective work is both desperate and inappropriate. The escalating specificity makes it cringier.

jokes.text: Well, you know, I've got art school. Oh, that's... Yeah, of course, you should totally do that. That's a great idea. Yeah, um... I am already. I started a while ago.
jokes.setup: Jim encouraging Pam about art school
jokes.punchline: Pam revealing she's already started, showing how out of touch they've become
jokes.explanation: Poignant character moment. Shows how Jim's absence has meant missing Pam's growth. The awkwardness of his overenthusiastic response to old news is perfectly played.

jokes.text: You can learn a great deal about a man from his trash. Case in point: Satellite TV bill. Hm, lesson learned: He's rich.
jokes.setup: Dwight going through the CFO's trash
jokes.punchline: His detective analysis that satellite TV means wealth
jokes.explanation: Classic Dwight pseudo-intellectual analysis. His formal tone for such basic deductions is funny. The 'case in point' format adds to his wannabe detective persona.

jokes.text: Coffee grounds. Was he enjoying a delicious hot beverage? Or disguising the scent of cocaine from drug-sniffing dogs?
jokes.setup: Dwight continuing his trash analysis
jokes.punchline: His leap from coffee grounds to cocaine conspiracy
jokes.explanation: Excellent Dwight escalation from mundane to conspiracy. The formal delivery of an insane theory is perfectly Dwight. The contrast between 'delicious hot beverage' and cocaine is brilliant.

jokes.text: How 'bout Cooper's, then? No seafood. Well, I don't want to go to DJ's. Oh, now, all of a sudden, you get picky? Okay. Forget it.
jokes.setup: Employees trying to agree on a restaurant for a farewell lunch
jokes.punchline: The typical group restaurant selection breakdown
jokes.explanation: Relatable group decision-making frustration. The escalation from reasonable suggestions to giving up is realistic workplace comedy.

jokes.text: Hooters. No.
jokes.setup: The restaurant selection debate
jokes.punchline: Creed's inappropriate suggestion and immediate rejection
jokes.explanation: Perfect Creed inappropriateness. The immediate 'No' response shows how predictably inappropriate his suggestion is. Economy makes it work.

jokes.text: Your branch is not closing. Stamford is closing. Um, for the time being, it seems that all of your jobs are safe.
jokes.setup: Everyone expecting their jobs to be lost
jokes.punchline: The complete reversal that they're actually safe and Stamford is closing
jokes.explanation: Major plot reversal with huge impact. The complete 180 from the episode's premise creates massive relief and surprise. Well-earned misdirection.

jokes.text: Is it because of Michael? Did he actually do something?
jokes.setup: The unexpected good news about the branch staying open
jokes.punchline: Pam's incredulous question about whether Michael actually helped
jokes.explanation: Great character-based humor. Pam's shock that Michael might have actually been effective is perfectly delivered. Shows everyone's low expectations of him.

jokes.text: Cornell has an extensive alumni network so... We look out for each other. Probably go back and teach or something. Where'd you go to college? Hm, Cornell.
jokes.setup: Andy explaining his backup plan
jokes.punchline: His repetitive Cornell references and Jim's deadpan response
jokes.explanation: Good Andy character establishment - his name-dropping and networking focus. Jim's patient response shows his professional demeanor at Stamford.

jokes.text: I just don't want it to be weird. You know? I mean, I took his old job and his old desk. Yeah, yeah, that might be weird.
jokes.setup: Discussion about Jim potentially returning
jokes.punchline: Ryan's oblivious comment about taking Jim's job and Pam's understated agreement
jokes.explanation: Good awkwardness about Jim's potential return. Ryan's practical concerns versus Pam's emotional ones. Her understated 'might be weird' hides deeper feelings.

jokes.text: We don't have to break up now, Ryan! Mm, mm, mm! It is the best day of my whole life.
jokes.setup: Kelly hearing about job security
jokes.punchline: Her joy about not having to break up with Ryan despite his obvious reluctance
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kelly obliviousness to Ryan's feelings. Her celebration of relationship continuation while he wanted to escape is darkly funny character work.

jokes.text: New York City is 45 minutes down the road from here. And you wanna move to Scranton.
jokes.setup: Jim considering the Scranton transfer
jokes.punchline: Karen pointing out the absurdity of choosing Scranton over New York proximity
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about geographic priorities. Karen's logical perspective contrasts with Jim's emotional decision-making about Pam.

jokes.text: Made like 1,200 bucks.
jokes.setup: Someone congratulating Creed
jokes.punchline: His casual mention of making money during the office chaos
jokes.explanation: Perfect Creed - mysterious, possibly illegal activity mentioned casually. The specificity of $1,200 makes it funnier and more suspicious.

jokes.text: Can you be at my place in 20 minutes? Yes, I can.
jokes.setup: Gary calling about their supposed 'deal' from years ago
jokes.punchline: Meredith's immediate agreement despite earlier confusion
jokes.explanation: Payoff to earlier sexual proposition confusion. Meredith's quick agreement shows she's more opportunistic than confused. Cringe comedy resolution.

jokes.text: Then it was an honor to have worked with you.
jokes.setup: Michael asking what if the office actually goes under
jokes.punchline: Dwight's unexpectedly formal and touching response
jokes.explanation: Beautiful character moment. Dwight's formal, almost military response shows his genuine loyalty to Michael. Unexpected emotional depth from the comedy relief character.

jokes.text: My first day, when you hazed me by spraying me with a fire extinguisher. That was hilarious. The foam.
jokes.setup: Michael asking for favorite Dunder Mifflin memories
jokes.punchline: Dwight fondly remembering what was clearly workplace harassment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight - viewing harassment as bonding. His genuine fondness for being mistreated shows his warped perspective and loyalty to Michael.

jokes.text: What about when Jan said the branch was closing? God, Dwight.
jokes.setup: Michael listing his favorite moments
jokes.punchline: Dwight innocently including the devastating news as a favorite moment
jokes.explanation: Good Dwight tone-deafness. Including the worst possible moment as a favorite memory shows his lack of emotional awareness. Michael's exasperated response is perfect.

jokes.text: Hey, I hear Jim's coming back. Really? Where did you hear that?
jokes.setup: Kevin casually mentioning Jim's return
jokes.punchline: Pam's careful response hiding her real interest
jokes.explanation: Good character interaction. Kevin's casual delivery contrasts with Pam's careful response. Shows her trying to seem disinterested while being very interested.

jokes.text: I'm, uh... I'm really glad you're still gonna be working here. Yeah, me, too. I mean, it'd... It's be a pain to have to find another job so...
jokes.setup: Jim and Pam's first real conversation since his return
jokes.punchline: Pam's deflection to practical concerns rather than emotional ones
jokes.explanation: Excellent awkward reunion. Both characters avoiding the real emotional content. Pam's deflection to job hunting practicalities shows her discomfort with deeper feelings.

jokes.text: I think you should take it. Okay, yeah. Maybe I will.
jokes.setup: Jim encouraging Pam to take the Scranton job if offered
jokes.punchline: Their stilted, overly polite interaction hiding complex feelings
jokes.explanation: Perfect awkwardness between characters with history. The overly casual tone masks their complicated feelings. Both trying to seem normal while being anything but.

jokes.text: Yeah, I'm... I'm happy he said that. I mean, I don't think he's into me or anything. But... I'm kind of into him. So... there you go.
jokes.setup: Pam's talking head about Jim's encouragement
jokes.punchline: Her admission that she's 'kind of into him' delivered as casual understatement
jokes.explanation: Major character development moment. Pam finally admitting her feelings, though with massive understatement ('kind of into him'). The casual 'there you go' delivery is perfect.

jokes.text: I'm such a stupid idiot. I let everybody down. Everybody hates me. I lost everybody's jobs. Nobody likes me anymore!
jokes.setup: Michael's despair about his failed mission
jokes.punchline: His spiral of self-pity with increasingly dramatic claims
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael self-pity spiral. The escalation from 'stupid idiot' to 'nobody likes me' is childish but believable for his character.

jokes.text: Stamford is closed! Michael, we're not closed. Stamford is closed! Stamford is closed! We did it? We did it! We did it!
jokes.setup: Michael getting the good news about Stamford closing instead
jokes.punchline: His immediate 180 from despair to manic celebration
jokes.explanation: Great payoff moment. Michael's instant emotional pivot from self-loathing to victory celebration is perfectly executed. The physical celebration adds to the comedy.

jokes.text: How did we do it? I don't know. I have no idea. I don't understand.
jokes.setup: Michael celebrating their 'victory'
jokes.punchline: His admission that he has no idea what they actually did
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael - taking credit for success he doesn't understand. The honest admission of confusion while still celebrating is classic Michael Scott logic.

jokes.text: Well, for a minute there, I saw myself selling my house, moving to Costa Rica, learning how to surf. But Costa Rica will still be there... when I'm 65.
jokes.setup: Michael reflecting on his brief despair
jokes.punchline: His practical deferral of his Costa Rica dreams until retirement
jokes.explanation: Great Michael fantasy followed by practical reality check. The specificity of Costa Rica and surfing, then the mundane 'when I'm 65' deflation is perfectly written.

tmdb_overview: Jan told Michael that the board had voted to close the Scranton branch. There would be a select group of employees that would get a severance package; the others would be going to work at the Stamford branch. She informed Michael that he would be one who would get a severance package.
